threats.title: Missing Cryptographic Validation Allows Arbitrary Quantity Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: The signature and Merkle proof validation in PhiFactory does not include the `quantity` or `imageURI` parameters in the signed payload or Merkle leaf. An attacker can reuse a valid signature or proof intended for 1 token to mint up to the `maxSupply`, bypassing intended limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cryptographic proofs must cover all user-controlled parameters that affect state. Since `quantity` is omitted from `claimData` and the Merkle leaf, the contract cannot distinguish between an authorized mint of 1 token and an unauthorized mint of 100 tokens using the same proof.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimData encodes (expiresIn_, minter_, ref_, verifier_, artId, block.chainid, data_) but omits quantity_

preconditions: Attacker has access to a valid signature or Merkle proof for at least 1 token
The target art has a maxSupply > 1

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Parameters passed to mintArgs are not included in the signed hash or Merkle leaf
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: quantity_ and imageURI_ can be arbitrarily modified by the caller
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No inclusion of quantity in claimData or leaf hashing

analysis_questions: Can an attacker successfully execute a claim with a modified quantity?
Does the mint fee calculation correctly charge the attacker for the inflated quantity?
validation_requirements: Construct a valid signature for quantity=1
Attempt to call signatureClaim with the same signature but quantity=100
Verify if the transaction succeeds and mints 100 tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker intercepts a valid signature for a credential mint. They call `signatureClaim` directly, passing the valid signature but changing the `mintArgs.quantity` to the remaining supply. They pay the required ETH fee and instantly mint all remaining NFTs, depriving other eligible users.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of intended scarcity, potential extraction of referral/verifier rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate users who are front-run or blocked from minting due to supply exhaustion

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying that signatures fail when quantity is modified

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient ETH to pay the mint fee for the inflated quantity
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time before the signature expires
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a single transaction minting a large quantity

threats.title: Public Access to Internal Cred Tracking Arrays Causes Permanent DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The functions `_addCredIdPerAddress` and `_removeCredIdPerAddress` in Cred.sol are declared `public` instead of `internal`. An attacker can call `_removeCredIdPerAddress` to corrupt a victim's position tracking, permanently preventing the victim from selling their shares.
threats.investigation_rationale: State-tracking arrays must only be modified by internal accounting logic. Exposing them publicly allows arbitrary manipulation of the indices, causing subsequent legitimate internal calls to revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Functions intended for internal state management are marked public

preconditions: Victim has bought shares and has an entry in `_credIdsPerAddress`

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Public visibility on state-mutating array management functions
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Attacker can delete the index mapping for a victim's credId

analysis_questions: Does calling `_removeCredIdPerAddress` successfully corrupt the victim's state?
Does the corrupted state cause `sellShareCred` to revert?
validation_requirements: User A buys a share
User B calls `_removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, User A)`
User A attempts to sell the share and observes the revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls `_removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim)`. The `_credIdsPerAddressCredIdIndex` mapping is deleted for that credId. When the victim tries to sell their last share, `_updateCuratorShareBalance` calls `_removeCredIdPerAddress` internally, which reverts with `WrongCredId` because the index is now stale. The victim's shares are permanently locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of user funds (shares and underlying ETH value)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user targeted by the attacker
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix state

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing access control tests for internal-use functions

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal gas for the attack transaction
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after the victim buys a share
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as direct calls to `_removeCredIdPerAddress`

threats.title: Excess ETH Sent to `claim` is Permanently Trapped in PhiFactory
threats.hypothesis_description: When a user calls `claim()` with `msg.value` greater than the required `mintFee`, the function forwards exactly `mintFee` to the internal `this.merkleClaim` call. The excess ETH remains in the `PhiFactory` contract and is never refunded to the user.
threats.investigation_rationale: The refund logic inside `_processClaim` uses the `msg.value` of the internal call (which is exactly `mintFee`), meaning `etherValue_ - mintFee` is always 0. The original caller's excess ETH is ignored and trapped.

proof_of_signal.pattern: this.merkleClaim{ value: mintFee } leaves excess msg.value in the contract

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Value truncation during external self-call without prior refund
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Refund logic in _processClaim only sees the truncated value
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No refund of `msg.value - mintFee` in the outer `claim` function

analysis_questions: Is the excess ETH permanently locked or can the user retrieve it?
Does `batchClaim` suffer from the exact same issue?
validation_requirements: Call `claim` with 1 ETH when `mintFee` is 0.1 ETH
Verify the user's balance decreases by 1 ETH and the factory balance increases by 0.9 ETH
Verify the user receives no refund

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user or frontend overestimates the required mint fee and sends excess ETH to `claim()`. The transaction succeeds, but the excess ETH is silently absorbed by the `PhiFactory` contract. The protocol owner can later extract this via `withdraw()`, effectively confiscating user funds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of user funds equal to the overpaid amount
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who sends excess ETH
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can withdraw and manually refund, but no trustless recovery

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for overpayment in `claim` and `batchClaim`

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as growing ETH balance in PhiFactory

threats.title: Attacker Can Permanently Lock Victim's Shares via `buyShareCredFor`
threats.hypothesis_description: The `buyShareCredFor` function allows anyone to buy shares for another address. This triggers `_handleTrade`, which sets `lastTradeTimestamp[credId][curator] = block.timestamp`. An attacker can repeatedly buy 1 share for a victim to continuously reset their lock period, preventing them from selling.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lock periods should only be reset by actions initiated by the user themselves. Allowing third parties to reset the lock period creates a cheap griefing vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lastTradeTimestamp[credId_][curator_] = block.timestamp; updates the victim's lock time

preconditions: Victim owns shares and intends to sell them

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Third-party state manipulation affecting time locks
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The `curator_` parameter is used instead of `msg.sender` for the lock timestamp
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that `msg.sender == curator_` when setting the lock

analysis_questions: Can the attacker bypass the lock period reset?
What is the economic cost to the attacker to sustain the lock?
validation_requirements: Victim buys a share
Wait 9 minutes
Attacker calls `buyShareCredFor` to buy 1 share for the victim
Wait 2 minutes
Victim attempts to sell and observes a revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A victim holds a large, profitable position in a credential. When they attempt to sell, an attacker front-runs them (or simply acts periodically) by calling `buyShareCredFor` to buy 1 share for the victim. The victim's `lastTradeTimestamp` is reset, and their sell transaction reverts. The attacker sustains this to trap the victim's capital.
prerequisites: Attacker is willing to pay the cost of 1 share periodically

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim capital is locked; attacker can manipulate market dynamics
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victim must use flashbots/private mempool to sell exactly when the lock expires before the attacker can reset it

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for third-party lock period resets

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Must execute before the victim's sell transaction
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as repeated small buys for a specific address

threats.title: Public `distribute` Function Sends Protocol Royalty to Caller
threats.hypothesis_description: The `distribute` function in `CuratorRewardsDistributor` calculates a `royaltyfee` (up to 10% of the total balance) and sends it directly to `_msgSender()`. Since the function is public, any MEV bot or user can call it to extract the protocol's intended royalty fee.
threats.investigation_rationale: Protocol fees should be sent to a designated protocol treasury. Sending them to `msg.sender` turns the fee into a public bounty, completely depriving the protocol of revenue and creating MEV spam.

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The calculated royalty fee is given to the caller instead of the protocol
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing access control or correct destination address for the royalty

analysis_questions: Is `withdrawRoyalty` intended as a keeper incentive or a protocol fee?
If it's an incentive, is 10% too high and vulnerable to MEV extraction?
validation_requirements: Deposit rewards into the distributor
Call `distribute` from an unrelated attacker account
Verify the attacker receives the 1% to 10% royalty fee

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A large amount of curator rewards accumulates for a credential. An attacker monitors the contract and calls `distribute(credId)`. The contract calculates the `royaltyfee` and transfers it to the attacker. The protocol loses its revenue.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 100% loss of protocol royalty revenue from curator distributions

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying the destination of the royalty fee

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as MEV bots calling distribute

threats.title: Creating Multiple Arts for the Same Cred Overwrites Merkle Root
threats.hypothesis_description: The Merkle root for a credential is stored in `credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId]`. If a creator deploys a second art for the same credential, the `_initializePhiArt` function overwrites this mapping with the new Merkle root, invalidating all unminted claims for the first art.
threats.investigation_rationale: Shared state across multiple distinct assets (arts) leads to state corruption when one asset is updated. The Merkle root should be scoped to the specific `artId`, not the shared `credId`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash; overwrites the shared mapping

preconditions: A creator deploys two different arts for the same `credId` with different Merkle roots

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State collision due to overly broad mapping keys
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check if the root is already set, or scoping by `artId`

analysis_questions: Can a creator intentionally grief users by updating the root?
Does this break cross-chain assumptions?
validation_requirements: Create Art A for Cred 1 with Root A
Create Art B for Cred 1 with Root B
Attempt to claim Art A using a valid proof for Root A
Verify the claim reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A creator launches an NFT collection (Art A) for a credential with a specific whitelist (Root A). Later, they launch a second collection (Art B) for the same credential with a different whitelist (Root B). The deployment of Art B overwrites the Merkle root, instantly breaking the ability of users to claim Art A.
prerequisites: Creator action to deploy a second art

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users lose the ability to claim NFTs they are entitled to
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Eligible minters of the original art
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Creator must deploy a third art with the original root to restore access

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for multiple arts sharing the same credId

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Cost to deploy a new art
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after the first art is deployed
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as root overwrites in state

threats.title: Unbounded Loop in `distribute` Causes Permanent Lock of Curator Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `distribute` function fetches all curators for a given `credId` and iterates over them to calculate and distribute rewards. For a popular credential with hundreds or thousands of curators, this loop will exceed the block gas limit, permanently locking all accumulated rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unbounded loops over dynamic user sets are a classic DoS vector. Since distribution cannot be paginated or pulled individually, the funds become permanently inaccessible once the gas limit is breached.
preconditions: A credential has a large number of unique curators

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded iteration over a dynamically growing array
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The array size is determined by the number of unique share buyers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pagination or pull-based withdrawal mechanism

analysis_questions: How many curators are required to hit the block gas limit?
Can an attacker intentionally bloat the curator list to trigger the DoS?
validation_requirements: Simulate 1000 unique curators buying 1 share each
Call `distribute` and measure gas consumption
Verify if it exceeds standard L2 block gas limits (e.g., 30M gas)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A credential becomes highly popular, attracting thousands of unique curators. When someone attempts to call `distribute` to distribute the accumulated ETH rewards, the transaction runs out of gas and reverts. The rewards are permanently locked in the contract.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all accumulated curator rewards for that credential
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All curators of the affected credential

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing gas limit tests for large curator sets

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Calculate gas per iteration to find the exact threshold
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None for natural occurrence; moderate for intentional bloat
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as out-of-gas reverts on distribute calls

threats.title: Pagination Bug in `getPositionsForCurator` Causes Reverts and Data Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: The `getPositionsForCurator` function uses the loop variable `i` (which starts at `start_`) to index into the newly allocated `credIds` and `amounts` arrays (which are sized `stopIndex - start_`). If `start_ > 0`, `i` will exceed the bounds of the arrays, causing a revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Incorrect array indexing in view functions breaks integrations and frontends that rely on pagination to read user state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credIds[i] = credId; amounts[i] = amount; where i can be >= credIds.length

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Using global loop index for a locally sized array
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The array is sized `stopIndex - start_`, but accessed at index `i` which starts at `start_`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use the `index` variable for array assignment

analysis_questions: Does the function revert for all paginated calls?
Does the assembly truncation at the end discard valid data when `start_ == 0` but entries are skipped?
validation_requirements: Call `getPositionsForCurator` with `start_ = 1` and `stop_ = 2`
Verify the call reverts with an out-of-bounds error

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A frontend or external smart contract attempts to paginate a user's large portfolio of credentials by calling `getPositionsForCurator(user, 10, 20)`. The call reverts due to an array out-of-bounds error, breaking the integration and preventing the user from viewing or interacting with their portfolio.
prerequisites: User has enough positions to require pagination

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but breaks core protocol functionality

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for pagination with start_ > 0

threats.title: `_getCreatorFee` Fails to Return Early, Causing Incorrect Price Quotes
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_getCreatorFee` function sets `creatorFee = 0` when `supply_ == 0` but lacks a `return` statement. Execution continues, and `creatorFee` is overwritten with a calculated fee. This causes `getBuyPriceAfterFee` to return inflated prices when the supply is zero.
threats.investigation_rationale: View functions must accurately reflect the state changes that will occur during execution. Returning inflated prices at zero supply misleads users and frontends, potentially discouraging initial purchases.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing early return in conditional block
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The `creatorFee` is recalculated and overwritten immediately after being set to 0

analysis_questions: Does this discrepancy affect actual trades or only view functions?
How large is the discrepancy for high royalty rates?
validation_requirements: Call `getBuyPriceAfterFee` with supply = 0
Compare the result with the actual cost charged by `buyShareCred` at supply = 0
Verify the view function returns a higher value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user views the price of the first share of a credential on the frontend. The frontend calls `getBuyPriceAfterFee`, which returns an inflated price due to the bug. The user decides not to buy, harming the credential's adoption. Actual trades are unaffected because `getPriceData` correctly returns 0.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of potential volume due to incorrect pricing display

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing view function consistency tests at edge cases (supply=0)

threats.title: Artists Can Retroactively Lock or Unlock NFT Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: The `safeTransferFrom` function checks the `soulBounded` status dynamically by querying `PhiFactory`. The `updateArtSettings` function allows the artist to change the `soulBounded` flag at any time, allowing them to retroactively make tradable NFTs soulbound or vice versa.
threats.investigation_rationale: Token transferability is a core property that users rely on when purchasing or minting. Allowing the creator to change this property after distribution violates user trust and asset ownership guarantees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: art.soulBounded = soulBounded_; can be updated after minting

preconditions: Artist calls `updateArtSettings` after tokens have been minted

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dynamic querying of mutable state for core token properties
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The `soulBounded` flag is not snapshotted at mint time

analysis_questions: Can this be used to rug-pull liquidity from secondary markets?
Does this break integrations with NFT marketplaces?
validation_requirements: Mint a non-soulbound token
Transfer it successfully
Artist updates settings to `soulBounded = true`
Attempt to transfer the token again and verify it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An artist launches a highly anticipated credential as tradable. Users mint and trade it on secondary markets, providing liquidity. The artist maliciously calls `updateArtSettings` to set `soulBounded = true`. All tokens are instantly frozen in their current wallets, destroying the secondary market value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of secondary market value for the affected NFTs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders of the specific credential

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for post-mint property mutation

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as an `ArtUpdated` event changing the `soulBounded` flag
